Fixing Mac XAMPP Apache Won't Start
XAMPP is a great one-stop PHP local dev tool, but recently MySQL was starting fine while Apache Web Server just hung. This post is my notes on what was going wrong.
Day 31 - Learning ThinkPHP From Scratch: The End Is Just Another Beginning

I picked Day 31 as the wrap-up for this series, so this post is mostly my closing thoughts on finishing the Ironman contest. I talk about why I was terrified of not making it to the finish line, why I chose ThinkPHP over Laravel or Yii, and I round things off with links to my other series so you've got somewhere to go next.
Day 30 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: Deploying to Heroku

Time to ship it. In this post I'll walk through deploying the ThinkPHP system we've been building over the last few chapters onto Heroku. We'll create a Heroku app, fix the inevitable 403, set up a ClearDB MySQL database, run our migrations and seeds, and patch a handful of deployment gotchas (missing CSS/JS, mb_substr, the captcha, and timestamps).
ThinkPHP CLI Commands and Database Migrations (Day 29)

Next up we'll get to know some of ThinkPHP's commonly used basic CLI commands. But the main focus of this post is migrations, because they'll affect how we work in the later chapters. We'll walk through the everyday commands, install think-migration, create migration files for our tables, write up() and down(), and finally seed the database with default data.
Day 28 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: Building a Message Board

We've covered most of the ThinkPHP basics by now, so this post walks through building a simple message board. I won't go deep into the code though — the focus here is on planning the message board, since it's basically just combining the techniques from earlier posts. I'll drop the GitHub repo at the end as a reference.
ThinkPHP Pagination: Built-in Pages and a Custom Bootstrap Style

Pagination is one of those features pretty much every site needs, and the good news is ThinkPHP ships with it out of the box. In this post we'll call paginate(), wire it up with some HTML, and get a working pager. Then we'll go a step further and override the default Bootstrap style with our own. Finally, I'll show you the cleaner way to load that custom paginator from the extend folder instead of hacking the core.
Day 26 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: Adding a Captcha

This post walks through ThinkPHP's built-in captcha feature. ThinkPHP ships captcha support out of the box, and unless you have some special requirement, the built-in one is more than enough. We'll add it to a form, wire up the validation error messages, and then build click-to-refresh and auto-refresh-every-60-seconds on top of it.
Stopping CSRF in ThinkPHP with Form Tokens

Now that form validation is out of the way, it's time to talk about form Tokens. A form Token only works alongside the validation we set up earlier, which is exactly why I saved it for after the validation chapter. Let's dig into what a form Token actually is and how it stops CSRF attacks in ThinkPHP.
Day 24 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: Form Validation (2)

Last time we got basic form validation working, but honestly that was only half the story. That's why I split this into two parts. In this one we'll send validation error messages back to the front end using ThinkPHP's flash feature, then simplify our template output with the View() helper. Let's keep going with form validation.

Whenever you submit a form, there's usually some kind of validation kicking in behind the scenes. So what about ThinkPHP? It turns out ThinkPHP ships with its own form validation feature, so in this post I'll walk through how to use ThinkPHP's built-in validation mechanism.
